title: Solution to Feynman's reverse sprinkler puzzle also applies to "silly sprinklers"
gist: NYU researchers used custom low-friction 'silly sprinkler' rigs to confirm their 2024 'momentum flux theory' of reverse-sprinkler rotation, resolving a century-old fluid dynamics puzzle and ruling out both Ernst Mach's and Richard Feynman's competing hypotheses, with potential relevance to turbine and flow-energy-conversion engineering.

themes.theme: Fluid dynamics research with engineering applications

insights.claim: NYU applied mathematician Leif Ristroph's lab confirmed experimentally that a reverse sprinkler rotates about 50 times slower than a forward-spraying sprinkler but operates via an analogous 'inside-out rocket' mechanism, validating their momentum flux theory over Mach's and Feynman's older hypotheses.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Not directly investable, but establishes a validated physical model of internal jet-collision flows that the researchers say can guide engineering of turbines and other devices converting fluid flow into energy.

insights.claim: Co-author Brennan Sprinkle of the Colorado School of Mines said the findings on how sprinkler arm geometry controls jet flow and torque can guide future engineering of flow-to-energy conversion devices such as turbines.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A basic-research signal worth filing for any thesis tracking academic fluid-dynamics work with potential downstream relevance to turbine/energy-conversion hardware design, though no near-term commercial application is stated.
insights.quote: Our findings provide a firmer understanding of how components respond to fluid flows—knowledge that can guide future engineering and technological advances for devices, such as turbines, that convert these flows into energy
insights.locator: closing paragraph, quote from Brennan Sprinkle
